title: Coil-built curved vase in air-dry clay
subtitle: A coiled vase that curves inward toward a narrowed neck
excerpt: A vase built from rolled clay coils, walked up the wall, and gradually curved inward toward a narrow neck. Air-dry clay sets at room temperature over several days, no kiln required. The finished vase is decorative; air-dry clay is porous and not water-tight.
sourceNotes: Coil-built vessels predate the wheel and appear in archaeological records across Africa, the Americas, and Asia. V&A and Wikimedia museum open-access photography for the historical-form reference. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim and join. It feels firm like thick leather.
Air-dried but unfired. The clay is pale and uniform in colour. For air-dry clay this is the finished structural state.
Clay diluted with water to a thick brushable consistency. Used as the bonding paste between joining surfaces.

content.text: A coil-built vase is one of the oldest pottery forms. Long rolled coils are stacked on a slab base, joined together, and walked into shape by hand. This vase curves outward from a flat base, reaches its widest point around mid-height, then curves back inward to a narrowed neck. Air-dry clay sets at room temperature over three to four days and the finished vase is decorative. Air-dry clay is porous; for cut flowers, use a glass test tube or florist tube inside the vase as a water-tight liner.

items.name: Air-dry clay
items.substitution: Paper clay air-dry for stronger joins

content.text: Preparation

Wedge the full 800 g of clay on the board for three or four minutes. The clay should feel uniform throughout with no soft pockets or dry crumbs. Keep the bulk wrapped in plastic between sessions; coil-building takes longer than a single sit-down and the clay must stay workable.
Building the vase
1. Make the base

Roll 100 g of clay into a ball, then flatten it under the heel of the hand to a disc 8 cm across and 10 mm thick. Smooth the top with a wooden rib. This is the base. Set it on the board and score the outer edge of the top surface lightly with the needle tool: a ring of cross-hatching around 5 mm wide where the first coil will sit.
2. Roll the first coil

Roll a snake of clay between the palms and the board until it is 12 mm thick and around 30 cm long. Even pressure rolls an even coil; pressing harder at one end produces a tapered coil that bunches as it joins. Brush
slip

onto the scored ring of the base and press the coil into place, walking it around to form a complete ring. Cut the ends at a slight angle with the fettling knife so they overlap and press them together. Smooth the join inside and out with a finger.
3. Add the next coils, curving outward

Roll the next coil to the same thickness. Score the top of the first coil and brush with slip. Place the second coil slightly outside the first so the wall flares outward by 2 to 3 mm. Press the coil down and outward, blending the inside down into the previous coil with the side of the finger or a wooden rib. Build to a height of around 10 cm in coils that step outward each time, so the vase reaches its widest point (around 13 cm diameter) at mid-height.
4. Curve the wall inward toward the neck

From mid-height upward, place each coil slightly inside the one below: by 2 mm at first, then 3 mm, then 4 mm. The wall begins to curve inward toward a neck. Smooth the inside more carefully than before: once the form narrows, the hand can no longer reach in to support the wall from inside. Aim for a finished wall thickness of 8 mm throughout.
5. Form the neck

Add three or four narrower coils to form the neck, around 6 cm diameter at the rim. The finished vase is around 22 cm tall. Smooth the rim with a damp sponge to round and soften the edge. Step back and check the silhouette from arm's length; an uneven curve reads as a mistake even when the seams are clean.
Working in stages
A vase this size cannot be built in one sitting. The lower wall must stiffen to
leather-hard

before the upper coils can be added without the whole form slumping under its own weight. Build to mid-height in the first session, cover loosely with plastic, and leave for four to six hours. Score and slip the top coil before adding the next; dry clay does not bond to dry clay, and the join will crack open if dry coil meets dry coil.
Smoothing

Once the vase is fully built and the wall has firmed to leather-hard, smooth the outside with the wooden rib in vertical strokes. The rib levels the coil bumps into a continuous surface. If the coiled texture is wanted instead, leave them visible. Press a damp sponge around the whole form to lift clay dust and round any sharp edges.
Drying

Cover loosely with plastic for the first twenty-four hours so the inside and outside dry at the same rate. Remove the plastic and turn the vase a quarter-turn every twelve hours so all sides dry evenly. Full drying to
bone-dry
takes three to four days at typical room humidity.
Finishing

Once bone-dry, sand the rim and any rough spots with 220-grit sandpaper. Do this outdoors or near an open window and wear a mask: dry clay dust is irritating to breathe. Paint with acrylics or leave plain, then seal with two thin coats of acrylic varnish brushed on cleanly. The seal protects the surface but does not make the vase water-tight.
Care

Air-dry clay is not waterproof and the vase will soften and crack if filled with water. For cut flowers, drop a glass test tube or florist water tube inside the neck and arrange the stems into that. Keep the vase indoors and away from damp surfaces.
Variations
Paper clay air-dry handles the long build better; the cellulose fibres bridge joins as they dry and reduce cracking.
Leave the coils visible as surface texture: instead of smoothing the outside flat with the rib, smooth only the inside and round the outside coil edges with the fingers.
Scale up: 1.5 kg of clay builds a vase around 30 cm tall. The wall thickness goes to 10 mm and the build takes one extra session.

items.symptom: Wall slumps outward as it gets taller
items.cause: Lower coils too wet to support the weight above
items.fix: Stop and let the lower wall firm to leather-hard for four to six hours under plastic; then resume

items.symptom: Crack opens along a coil join during drying
items.cause: Surfaces too dry when joined, or slip omitted
items.fix: Score deeper and use thicker slip; rescue an open crack by pressing fresh slip into it and drying under plastic for twelve hours

items.cause: Coils placed unevenly around the wall
items.fix: Check the silhouette every two coils from arm's length; pinch out a bulge or add an extra short coil to fill a dip while the clay is still soft

items.symptom: Inside of the neck rough where the hand could not reach
items.cause: Inside not smoothed before the form narrowed
items.fix: Smooth the inside of every coil before placing the next; once the neck narrows it cannot be reached. A long wooden spoon handle helps once the opening is too small for the hand
